{{One source|date=2023年6月}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha nishihongu-honden.jpg|thumb|right|300px|日吉大社]] '''山王信仰'''（さんのうしんこう）とは、[[比叡山]]麓の[[日吉大社]]（[[滋賀県]][[大津市]]）より生じた[[神道]]の信仰である。
== 用語 == 山王とは、滋賀県大津市[[坂本 (大津市)|坂本]]の日吉大社で祀られる神の別名であり、比叡山に鎮まる神を指したものである。
'''[[日吉神社]]'''・'''[[日枝神社]]'''（ひよしじんじゃ、ひえじんじゃ）あるいは'''山王神社'''などという社名の[[神社]]は山王信仰に基づいて日吉大社を[[分霊|勧請]]した神社で、[[大山咋神]]と[[大物主|大物主神]]（または[[大国主|大国主神]]）を祭神とし、日本全国に約3,800社ある。
[[神仏習合]]期には「[[山王権現]]」や「日吉山王」とも称され、今日でも'''山王さん'''の愛称で親しまれている。なお、日吉大社では[[サル|猿]]を[[神使]]とするが、猿との関連性についてはよく分かっていない。おそらくは原始信仰の名残りではないかと推測されている。
== 歴史 == === 日吉大社の創建と延暦寺 === 日吉大社は、もともと近江国日枝山（ひえのやま：後に[[比叡山]]の字が充てられた）の神である「大山咋神」（おおやまくいのかみ）を祀っていたもので、後に[[近江京]]遷都の翌年である[[天智天皇]]七年（[[668年]]）、大津京鎮護のためYamato Province [[三輪山]]（三諸山（みもろやま）とも）の[[大三輪神]]（おおみわのかみ）、すなわち[[大物主神]]（おおものぬしのかみ）を[[勧請]]しともに祀られた。
比叡山に[[天台宗]]の[[延暦寺]]ができてからは、大山咋神・大物主神は地主神として天台宗・延暦寺の守護神とされた。延暦寺を開いた最澄は寺の周囲に結界を定め、その地主神を比叡山の「諸山王」として比叡社に祀った<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html 『日吉大社 山王三聖の形成 ＜最澄・円澄・円珍・良源の山王観の変遷＞』（江頭務、イワクラ（磐座）学会会報28号、2013年7月12日）]</ref>。[[唐]]の[[天台山]]国清寺が[[地主神]]として「[[山王弼真君]]」を祀っていることに因み、延暦寺ではこの両神を「山王」と称した。なお、最澄にとって比叡山の「山王」とは、山岳信仰に基づく、アニミズム的な形態に近い信仰対象であった<ref name="iwakura" />。
最澄にとって、「山王」とは山の地主神を仏教的に表現したものであるといわれる<ref name="iwakura" />。最澄が著したと思われる文書には、神名ではなくほとんど「山王」が使われており、あえて「神」とは呼ばなかった点に、仏教徒としての配慮がうかがわれるという<ref name="iwakura" />。このような、最澄による「山王」の扱いが、後に神仏習合の「山王神道」の成立を導いていったともされる<ref name="iwakura" />。
=== 両所三聖 === [[天長]]2年（[[825年]]）に、天台宗の第2代座主である[[円澄]]が、延暦寺の西塔を開いた<ref name="iwakura" />。以後、西塔は独自色を深め、それまでの「東塔」での地主神信仰に対応させるかたちで、小比叡神を祀るようになった<ref name="iwakura" />。小比叡神には、八王子山の磐座の神である大山咋神が勧請され、小比叡峯にある磐座に神が宿るとされた<ref name="iwakura" />。
西塔の独立により、最澄が当初祀った諸山王は統合され、東塔と結びついて、比叡神または大比叡神と呼ばれるようになった<ref name="iwakura" />。西塔で祀られる神も山王と呼ばれたため、「山王」は東塔と西塔で二極化することとなった<ref name="iwakura" />。
その後、天台宗の第5代座主であり、夢で様々な啓示を受けたという伝承が残されている[[円珍]]が、円珍に夢で入唐を勧めたとされる「山王明神」を、自身の坊に祀るようになった<ref name="iwakura" />。このため、それまでは最澄の創建として、千手堂または千手院と呼ばれていた円珍の坊が、山王院と呼ばれるようになった<ref name="iwakura" />。このように、山王明神の信仰は、円珍が個人的に祀ったことから始まった<ref name="iwakura" />。
こうして、大比叡神（東塔）・小比叡神（西塔）・比叡山王（山王明神）の「両所三聖」が成立した<ref name="iwakura" />。なお、円珍にとっては、「両所三聖」の中でも山王（山王明神）は別格で、「両所二聖」を超える存在、つまり、大比叡神・小比叡神を含んだ「比叡山」そのものを象徴しており、最澄が祀った諸山王をひとつにまとめた、非常に大きな信仰対象であったといわれる<ref name="iwakura" />。
=== 地主三聖 === 延暦寺の第18代座主であり、比叡山中興の祖とされる[[良源]]は、[[天禄]]3年（[[972年]]）に、比叡山の「横川」を、東塔・西塔に匹敵する地位を持つ独立地区として認めた<ref name="iwakura" />。もともと、横川の発展には良源が大きく関わっており、その独立の裏にも、良源の意向があったとされる<ref name="iwakura" />。以後、良源の意向は、古くから存在する東塔・西塔よりも、横川に大きく影響するようになった<ref name="iwakura" />。
独立した横川は、西塔と同じように独自の地主神を求め、聖真子（しょうしんし）を信仰するようになった<ref name="iwakura" />。聖真子の信仰は、既に、[[康保]]5年（[[968年]]）に認められるとされ、「聖真子」の名は法華経により、正統な仏法の後継者を意味するもので、神名であると同時に法号であり、日本古来の神々の系譜から切り離された独自のものとされるなど、神仏習合の最たるかたちを示しているとされる<ref name="iwakura" />。
ここに、良源の思惑により、大比叡神（東塔）・小比叡神（西塔）・聖真子（横川）の「地主三聖」が成立した<ref name="iwakura" />。
だが、円珍によって定められた「両所三聖」を信仰していた僧たちは、良源に導かれて成立した「地主三聖」の信仰に反発することとなった<ref name="iwakura" />。良源は「地主三聖」の信仰に反対する僧たちを僧籍から除名するなどし、後の山門・寺門分裂への流れを生み出していくこととなる<ref name="iwakura" />。
=== 地主三聖から山王三聖へ === 良源により「地主三聖」の信仰が定着するにつれ、「地主三聖」は徐々に「山王三聖」と呼ばれるようになっていった<ref name="iwakura" />。なお、「地主山王」と呼ばれた時期もあった<ref name="iwakura" />。「地主三聖」の語は三塔の存在を意識して、それぞれの「地主」を強調しているが、「山王三聖」の「山王」の語は、寺院というより、比叡山という山全体に関わる神を意識しているとされる<ref name="iwakura" />。
特に、[[正暦]]4年（[[993年]]）の叡山分裂以降、「山王三聖」の語は定着していったとされる<ref name="iwakura" />。なお、「山王三聖」の語が文献に現れた最初は、康保5年（968年）の太政官牒であるとされる<ref name="iwakura" />。
なお、円珍が個人的に祀った「山王（山王明神）」とは、円珍が実際に夢でお告げを受けたということから、確かに「実在」すると信じられた神を指したものであるが、良源の場合、「山王」の語には確かに実在するという重みはなく、ただ「地主三聖」の総称に過ぎず、抽象的な概念にとどまっていたとされる<ref name="iwakura" />。
=== 山王信仰の発展 === 天台宗が全国に広がる過程で、山王信仰に基づいて日吉社も全国に勧請・創建された。日吉（ひよし）神社・日枝（ひえ）神社、あるいは山王神社などという社名の神社は、日本全国に約3,800社ある。これにともない、日吉・日枝・比恵・山王・坂本などという地名が各地でみられる。
=== 本地仏制定 === 山王三聖には、本地仏として、大宮（大比叡神、東塔）に釈迦如来、二宮（小比叡神、西塔）に薬師如来、聖真子（横川）に阿弥陀如来が、それぞれ定められた。これらの本地仏が定着したのは、浄土教の影響により八幡神（聖真子と同一とされる）の本地仏が釈迦から阿弥陀に変わった11世紀以降と推定される<ref name="iwakura" />。本地仏の制定について、それぞれの由来には諸説があるが、定説といえるものはないとされる<ref name="iwakura" />。本地仏の制定は、日吉大社における、本格的な神仏習合の始まりであるともいわれる<ref name="iwakura" />。
やがて八王子(本地[[千手観音]])、客人(本地[[十一面観音]])、十禅師(本地[[地蔵菩薩|地蔵菩醍]])、三宮(本地[[普賢菩薩|普賢菩醍]])を加えて山王七社（上七社）となすなど、総本山の威容を整え始めた。そして[[本地垂迹説]]によってさらに数を増し中七社、下七社を加えて「山王二十一社」と称した。
=== 山王神道 === 山王信仰は、「[[山王神道]]」とも呼ばれる信仰をも派生させた。山王神道では山王神は[[釈迦]]の垂迹であるとされ、「山」の字も「王」の字も、三本の線とそれを貫く一本の線からなっており、これを天台宗の思想である[[三諦即一思想]]と結びつけて説いた。また天台密教は、[[鎮護国家]]、[[増益延命]]、[[息災]]といった具体的な[[霊験]]を[[加持祈祷]]によって実現するという体系（使命）を持ち、山王にも「[[現世利益]]」を実現する霊威と呪力を高める性格を与えたようである。
=== 中世以降 === 中世に比叡山の[[僧兵]]が[[強訴]]のために担ぎ出した神輿は日吉大社のものである。
[[元亀]]2年（[[1571年]]）、[[織田信長]]の[[比叡山焼き討ち (1571年)|比叡山焼き討ち]]により、日吉大社も灰燼に帰した。現在見られる建造物は、安土桃山時代以降に再建されたものである。
以下に現在の日吉神社の有力社殿（山王二十一社）を列記する。（ ）内は旧称。
;上七社（山王七社） *西本宮（大宮（大比叡））大己貴神 *東本宮（二宮（小比叡））大山咋神 *宇佐宮（聖真子）田心姫神 *牛尾神社（八王子）大山咋神荒魂 *白山姫神社（客人）白山姫神 *樹下神社（十禅師）鴨玉依姫神 *三宮神社（三宮）鴨玉依姫神荒魂
;中七社 *大物忌神社（大行事）大年神 *牛御子社（牛御子）山末之大主神荒魂 *新物忌神社（新行事）天知迦流水姫神 *八柱社（下八王子）五男三女神 *早尾神社（早尾）素盞嗚神 *産屋神社（王子）鴨別雷神 *宇佐若宮（聖女）下照姫神
;下七社 *樹下若宮（小禅師）玉依彦神 *竈殿社（大宮竈殿）奥津彦神・奥津姫神 *竈殿社（二宮竈殿）奥津彦神・奥津姫神 *氏神神社（山末）鴨建角身命・琴御館宇志麿 *巌滝社（岩滝）市杵島姫命・湍津島姫命 *剣宮社（剣宮）瓊々杵命 *気比社（気比）仲哀天皇
なお[[江戸]]で「三大祭」として賑わったのは、[[山王祭 (千代田区)|山王祭]]（さんのうまつり）、[[神田祭]]、[[深川祭]]であるが、この山王祭は、[[徳川家康]]が江戸に移封された際に、同地にあった日吉社を城内の紅葉山に遷座し、[[江戸城]]の[[鎮守]]としたことに始まる。この社の由来は、[[太田道灌]]が江戸城築城にあたり、[[Bunmei]]10年（[[1478年]]）に川越の無量寿寺（現在の[[喜多院]]）の鎮守である日吉社を勧請したのに始まるとされる。無量寿寺は平安初期の[[天長]]7年（[[830年]]）、[[淳和天皇]]の命で[[円仁]]（慈覚大師）が建立したとされる。ちなみに、この江戸の日吉社に日枝（ひえ）神社と名称が付けられたのは、[[慶応]]4年（明治元年）6月11日以降のことである。（[[神仏分離]]）
天台宗が全国に広がる過程で、山王権現も各地に[[勧請]]され、多くは天台宗の寺院の鎮守神とされた。[[明治]]の[[神仏分離]]の際に、[[仏教]]色を廃し寺院とは別れた。
社名については、「日吉」と書いて「ひえ」と読むもの、「日吉」と書いて「ひよし」と読むもの、「日枝」と書いて「ひえ」と読むものがある。

{{日本天台宗}} {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:さんのうしんこう}} [[Category:山王信仰|!]] [[Category:神仏習合]] [[Category:日吉大社]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Sannō Faith]][[Category:11]] [[sn:山王信仰]][[ja:山王信仰]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages to be translated]][[sn:Sannō Faith]][[Category:pages to be translated]] [[Category:pages to be translated]][[sn:Sannō Faith]][[Category:pages to be translated]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Sannō Faith}}

{{One source|date=2023年6月}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha nishihongu-honden.jpg|thumb|right|300px|日吉大社]] '''山王信仰'''（さんのうしんこう）とは、[[比叡山]]麓の[[日吉大社]]（[[滋賀県]][[大津市]]）より生じた[[神道]]の信仰である。
== 用語 == 山王とは、滋賀県大津市[[坂本 (大津市)|坂本]]の日吉大社で祀られる神の別名であり、比叡山に鎮まる神を指したものである。
'''[[日吉神社]]'''・'''[[日枝神社]]'''（ひよしじんじゃ、ひえじんじゃ）あるいは'''山王神社'''などという社名の[[神社]]は山王信仰に基づいて日吉大社を[[分霊|勧請]]した神社で、[[大山咋神]]と[[大物主|大物主神]]（または[[大国主|大国主神]]）を祭神とし、日本全国に約3,800社ある。
[[神仏習合]]期には「[[山王権現]]」や「日吉山王」とも称され、今日でも'''山王さん'''の愛称で親しまれている。なお、日吉大社では[[サル|猿]]を[[神使]]とするが、猿との関連性についてはよく分かっていない。おそらくは原始信仰の名残りではないかと推測されている。
== 歴史 == === 日吉大社の創建と延暦寺 === 日吉大社は、もともと近江国日枝山（ひえのやま：後に[[比叡山]]の字が充てられた）の神である「大山咋神」（おおやまくいのかみ）を祀っていたもので、後に[[近江京]]遷都の翌年である[[天智天皇]]七年（[[668年]]）、大津京鎮護のためYamato Province [[三輪山]]（三諸山（みもろやま）とも）の[[大三輪神]]（おおみわのかみ）、すなわち[[大物主神]]（おおものぬしのかみ）を[[勧請]]しともに祀られた。
比叡山に[[天台宗]]の[[延暦寺]]ができてからは、大山咋神・大物主神は地主神として天台宗・延暦寺の守護神とされた。延暦寺を開いた最澄は寺の周囲に結界を定め、その地主神を比叡山の「諸山王」として比叡社に祀った<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html 『日吉大社 山王三聖の形成 ＜最澄・円澄・円珍・良源の山王観の変遷＞』（江頭務、イワクラ（磐座）学会会報28号、2013年7月12日）]</ref>。[[唐]]の[[天台山]]国清寺が[[地主神]]として「[[山王弼真君]]」を祀っていることに因み、延暦寺ではこの両神を「山王」と称した。なお、最澄にとって比叡山の「山王」とは、山岳信仰に基づく、アニミズム的な形態に近い信仰対象であった<ref name="iwakura" />。
最澄にとって、「山王」とは山の地主神を仏教的に表現したものであるといわれる<ref name="iwakura" />。最澄が著したと思われる文書には、神名ではなくほとんど「山王」が使われており、あえて「神」とは呼ばなかった点に、仏教徒としての配慮がうかがわれるという<ref name="iwakura" />。このような、最澄による「山王」の扱いが、後に神仏習合の「山王神道」の成立を導いていったともされる<ref name="iwakura" />。
=== 両所三聖 === [[天長]]2年（[[825年]]）に、天台宗の第2代座主である[[円澄]]が、延暦寺の西塔を開いた<ref name="iwakura" />。以後、西塔は独自色を深め、それまでの「東塔」での地主神信仰に対応させるかたちで、小比叡神を祀るようになった<ref name="iwakura" />。小比叡神には、八王子山の磐座の神である大山咋神が勧請され、小比叡峯にある磐座に神が宿るとされた<ref name="iwakura" />。
西塔の独立により、最澄が当初祀った諸山王は統合され、東塔と結びついて、比叡神または大比叡神と呼ばれるようになった<ref name="iwakura" />。西塔で祀られる神も山王と呼ばれたため、「山王」は東塔と西塔で二極化することとなった<ref name="iwakura" />。
その後、天台宗の第5代座主であり、夢で様々な啓示を受けたという伝承が残されている[[円珍]]が、円珍に夢で入唐を勧めたとされる「山王明神」を、自身の坊に祀るようになった<ref name="iwakura" />。このため、それまでは最澄の創建として、千手堂または千手院と呼ばれていた円珍の坊が、山王院と呼ばれるようになった<ref name="iwakura" />。このように、山王明神の信仰は、円珍が個人的に祀ったことから始まった<ref name="iwakura" />。
こうして、大比叡神（東塔）・小比叡神（西塔）・比叡山王（山王明神）の「両所三聖」が成立した<ref name="iwakura" />。なお、円珍にとっては、「両所三聖」の中でも山王（山王明神）は別格で、「両所二聖」を超える存在、つまり、大比叡神・小比叡神を含んだ「比叡山」そのものを象徴しており、最澄が祀った諸山王をひとつにまとめた、非常に大きな信仰対象であったといわれる<ref name="iwakura" />。
=== 地主三聖 === 延暦寺の第18代座主であり、比叡山中興の祖とされる[[良源]]は、[[天禄]]3年（[[972年]]）に、比叡山の「横川」を、東塔・西塔に匹敵する地位を持つ独立地区として認めた<ref name="iwakura" />。もともと、横川の発展には良源が大きく関わっており、その独立の裏にも、良源の意向があったとされる<ref name="iwakura" />。以後、良源の意向は、古くから存在する東塔・西塔よりも、横川に大きく影響するようになった<ref name="iwakura" />。
独立した横川は、西塔と同じように独自の地主神を求め、聖真子（しょうしんし）を信仰するようになった<ref name="iwakura" />。聖真子の信仰は、既に、[[康保]]5年（[[968年]]）に認められるとされ、「聖真子」の名は法華経により、正統な仏法の後継者を意味するもので、神名であると同時に法号であり、日本古来の神々の系譜から切り離された独自のものとされるなど、神仏習合の最たるかたちを示しているとされる<ref name="iwakura" />。
ここに、良源の思惑により、大比叡神（東塔）・小比叡神（西塔）・聖真子（横川）の「地主三聖」が成立した<ref name="iwakura" />。
だが、円珍によって定められた「両所三聖」を信仰していた僧たちは、良源に導かれて成立した「地主三聖」の信仰に反発することとなった<ref name="iwakura" />。良源は「地主三聖」の信仰に反対する僧たちを僧籍から除名するなどし、後の山門・寺門分裂への流れを生み出していくこととなる<ref name="iwakura" />。
=== 地主三聖から山王三聖へ === 良源により「地主三聖」の信仰が定着するにつれ、「地主三聖」は徐々に「山王三聖」と呼ばれるようになっていった<ref name="iwakura" />。なお、「地主山王」と呼ばれた時期もあった<ref name="iwakura" />。「地主三聖」の語は三塔の存在を意識して、それぞれの「地主」を強調しているが、「山王三聖」の「山王」の語は、寺院というより、比叡山という山全体に関わる神を意識しているとされる<ref name="iwakura" />。
特に、[[正暦]]4年（[[993年]]）の叡山分裂以降、「山王三聖」の語は定着していったとされる<ref name="iwakura" />。なお、「山王三聖」の語が文献に現れた最初は、康保5年（968年）の太政官牒であるとされる<ref name="iwakura" />。
なお、円珍が個人的に祀った「山王（山王明神）」とは、円珍が実際に夢でお告げを受けたということから、確かに「実在」すると信じられた神を指したものであるが、良源の場合、「山王」の語には確かに実在するという重みはなく、ただ「地主三聖」の総称に過ぎず、抽象的な概念にとどまっていたとされる<ref name="iwakura" />。
=== 山王信仰の発展 === 天台宗が全国に広がる過程で、山王信仰に基づいて日吉社も全国に勧請・創建された。日吉（ひよし）神社・日枝（ひえ）神社、あるいは山王神社などという社名の神社は、日本全国に約3,800社ある。これにともない、日吉・日枝・比恵・山王・坂本などという地名が各地でみられる。
=== 本地仏制定 === 山王三聖には、本地仏として、大宮（大比叡神、東塔）に釈迦如来、二宮（小比叡神、西塔）に薬師如来、聖真子（横川）に阿弥陀如来が、それぞれ定められた。これらの本地仏が定着したのは、浄土教の影響により八幡神（聖真子と同一とされる）の本地仏が釈迦から阿弥陀に変わった11世紀以降と推定される<ref name="iwakura" />。本地仏の制定について、それぞれの由来には諸説があるが、定説といえるものはないとされる<ref name="iwakura" />。本地仏の制定は、日吉大社における、本格的な神仏習合の始まりであるともいわれる<ref name="iwakura" />。
やがて八王子(本地[[千手観音]])、客人(本地[[十一面観音]])、十禅師(本地[[地蔵菩薩|地蔵菩醍]])、三宮(本地[[普賢菩薩|普賢菩醍]])を加えて山王七社（上七社）となすなど、総本山の威容を整え始めた。そして[[本地垂迹説]]によってさらに数を増し中七社、下七社を加えて「山王二十一社」と称した。
=== 山王神道 === 山王信仰は、「[[山王神道]]」とも呼ばれる信仰をも派生させた。山王神道では山王神は[[釈迦]]の垂迹であるとされ、「山」の字も「王」の字も、三本の線とそれを貫く一本の線からなっており、これを天台宗の思想である[[三諦即一思想]]と結びつけて説いた。また天台密教は、[[鎮護国家]]、[[増益延命]]、[[息災]]といった具体的な[[霊験]]を[[加持祈祷]]によって実現するという体系（使命）を持ち、山王にも「[[現世利益]]」を実現する霊威と呪力を高める性格を与えたようである。
=== 中世以降 === 中世に比叡山の[[僧兵]]が[[強訴]]のために担ぎ出した神輿は日吉大社のものである。
[[元亀]]2年（[[1571年]]）、[[織田信長]]の[[比叡山焼き討ち (1571年)|比叡山焼き討ち]]により、日吉大社も灰燼に帰した。現在見られる建造物は、安土桃山時代以降に再建されたものである。
以下に現在の日吉神社の有力社殿（山王二十一社）を列記する。（ ）内は旧称。
;上七社（山王七社） *西本宮（大宮（大比叡））大己貴神 *東本宮（二宮（小比叡））大山咋神 *宇佐宮（聖真子）田心姫神 *牛尾神社（八王子）大山咋神荒魂 *白山姫神社（客人）白山姫神 *樹下神社（十禅師）鴨玉依姫神 *三宮神社（三宮）鴨玉依姫神荒魂
;中七社 *大物忌神社（大行事）大年神 *牛御子社（牛御子）山末之大主神荒魂 *新物忌神社（新行事）天知迦流水姫神 *八柱社（下八王子）五男三女神 *早尾神社（早尾）素盞嗚神 *産屋神社（王子）鴨別雷神 *宇佐若宮（聖女）下照姫神
;下七社 *樹下若宮（小禅師）玉依彦神 *竈殿社（大宮竈殿）奥津彦神・奥津姫神 *竈殿社（二宮竈殿）奥津彦神・奥津姫神 *氏神神社（山末）鴨建角身命・琴御館宇志麿 *巌滝社（岩滝）市杵島姫命・湍津島姫命 *剣宮社（剣宮）瓊々杵命 *気比社（気比）仲哀天皇
なお[[江戸]]で「三大祭」として賑わったのは、[[山王祭 (千代田区)|山王祭]]（さんのうまつり）、[[神田祭]]、[[深川祭]]であるが、この山王祭は、[[徳川家康]]が江戸に移封された際に、同地にあった日吉社を城内の紅葉山に遷座し、[[江戸城]]の[[鎮守]]としたことに始まる。この社の由来は、[[太田道灌]]が江戸城築城にあたり、[[Bunmei]]10年（[[1478年]]）に川越の無量寿寺（現在の[[喜多院]]）の鎮守である日吉社を勧請したのに始まるとされる。無量寿寺は平安初期の[[天長]]7年（[[830年]]）、[[淳和天皇]]の命で[[円仁]]（慈覚大師）が建立したとされる。ちなみに、この江戸の日吉社に日枝（ひえ）神社と名称が付けられたのは、[[慶応]]4年（明治元年）6月11日以降のことである。（[[神仏分離]]）
天台宗が全国に広がる過程で、山王権現も各地に[[勧請]]され、多くは天台宗の寺院の鎮守神とされた。[[明治]]の[[神仏分離]]の際に、[[仏教]]色を廃し寺院とは別れた。
社名については、「日吉」と書いて「ひえ」と読むもの、「日吉」と書いて「ひよし」と読むもの、「日枝」と書いて「ひえ」と読むものがある。
== 各地の日吉神社・日枝神社 == {{Main|山王神社|日吉神社|日枝神社}} === 総本社 === *[[日吉大社]]（[[滋賀県]][[大津市]]、旧官幣大社、別表神社）
=== 別表神社・旧官国幣社 === *[[日枝神社 (千代田区)|日枝神社]]（[[東京都]][[千代田区]]、旧官幣大社、別表神社） *[[日枝神社 (富山市)|日枝神社]]（[[富山県]][[富山市]]、旧県社、別表神社）
=== その他の神社 === <!--府県社のみ--> *[[日吉神社 (神戸町)|日吉神社]]（[[岐阜県]][[安八郡]][[神戸町]]、旧県社） *[[日枝神社 (高山市)|日枝神社]]（[[岐阜県]][[高山市]]、旧県社） *[[日枝神社 (酒田市)|日枝神社]]（[[山形県]][[酒田市]]、旧県社） *[[日枝神社 (桐生市)|日枝神社]]（[[群馬県]][[桐生市]][[梅田町 (桐生市)|梅田町]]、旧県社） *[[日吉八幡神社]]（[[秋田県]][[秋田市]]、旧県社） 日吉神社 【愛知県清須市】 *[[新日吉神宮]]（[[京都府]][[京都市]][[東山区]]、旧府社） *[[日吉神社 (柳川市)|日吉神社]]（[[福岡県]][[柳川市]]、旧県社） *[[日吉神社 (熊本市)|日吉神社]]（[[熊本県]][[熊本市]]、旧県社） *[[山王神社 (長崎市)|山王神社]]（[[長崎県]][[長崎市]]、旧県社） 日吉神社（大崎市）[宮城県]

{{One source|date=June 2023}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha nishihongu-honden.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine]] '''Sannō Faith''' (山王信仰) is a [[Shinto]] belief originating from the [[Hiyoshi Taisha]] located at the foot of [[Mount Hiei]] ([[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu City]], [[Shiga Prefecture]]).
== Terminology == Sannō refers to another name for the deity worshipped at Hiyoshi Taisha in [[Sakamoto, Shiga|Sakamoto]], Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture, specifically indicating the deity enshrined on Mount Hiei.
Shrines named '''[[Hiyoshi Shrine]]''', '''[[Hie Shrine]]''', or '''Sannō Shrine''' across Japan are derived from Sannō Faith, and they enshrine the divided spirits ({{ill|Bunrei|ja|分霊}}) of Hiyoshi Taisha. These shrines primarily worship {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} (or {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}), totaling about 3,800 nationwide.
During the {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} period, it was also known as '{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}}' or 'Hiyoshi Sannō,' and is affectionately called '''Sannō-san''' even today. Although Hiyoshi Taisha uses monkeys ({{ill|saru|ja|サル}}) as divine messengers, the exact relation with monkeys is not well understood and may reflect remnants of primitive worship.
== History == === Establishment of Hiyoshi Taisha and Enryaku-ji === Hiyoshi Taisha originally enshrined {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神}}, deity of Mount Hie (later named [[Mount Hiei]]). After the relocation of the capital to {{ill|Ōmi-kyō|ja|近江京}}, in the seventh year of Emperor Tenji ([[668]]), {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kami|ja|大三輪神}} (Ōmononushi no Kami) was enshrined from Mount Miwa (Yamato Province) to protect the new capital.
When the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monastery of [[Enryaku-ji]] was founded on Mount Hiei, these deities became the local guardian deities. Saichō, founder of Enryaku-ji, set these local deities as the 'Various Sannō' of Mount Hiei.<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō of Hiyoshi Taisha] (Tsutomu Egashira, Iwakura Society Bulletin No. 28, July 12, 2013)</ref> The term "Sannō" was adopted following the deity "Sannō Hitsu Shinkun" enshrined at the {{ill|Tiantai Mountain|ja|天台山}} Guoqing Temple in Tang China.<ref name="iwakura" />
Saichō referred to "Sannō" as a Buddhist expression of local mountain deities, avoiding explicitly calling them "kami".<ref name="iwakura" /> This approach laid the foundation for the Shinbutsu-shūgō (syncretic) "Sannō Shintō".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Ryōsho Sansei === In [[825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch {{ill|Ryōgen|ja|良源}} elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
== Hiyoshi/Hie Shrines Nationwide == {{Main|Sannō Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine|Hie Shrine}} === Head Shrine === * [[Hiyoshi Taisha]]
=== Notable Shrines === * [[Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|Hie Shrine]] (Tokyo) * [[Hie Shrine (Toyama)|Hie Shrine]] (Toyama)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == *{{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html |title=Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō (Tsutomu Egashira)|date=20140714221329}}
== See Also == *[[Sannō Shintō]] *[[Hiyoshi Taisha]] *[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]

=== Ryōsho Sansei === In [[825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch {{ill|Ryōgen|ja|良源}} elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.

=== Notable Shrines === * [[Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|Hie Shrine]] (Tokyo) * [[Hie Shrine (Toyama)|Hie Shrine]] (Toyama)

revision.text:
{{One source|date=June 2023}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha nishihongu-honden.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine]] '''Sannō Faith''' (山王信仰) is a [[Shinto]] belief originating from the [[Hiyoshi Taisha]] located at the foot of [[Mount Hiei]] ([[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu City]], [[Shiga Prefecture]]).
== Terminology == Sannō refers to another name for the deity worshipped at Hiyoshi Taisha in [[Sakamoto, Shiga|Sakamoto]], Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture, specifically indicating the deity enshrined on Mount Hiei.
Shrines named '''[[Hiyoshi Shrine]]''', '''[[Hie Shrine]]''', or '''Sannō Shrine''' across Japan are derived from Sannō Faith, and they enshrine the divided spirits ({{ill|Bunrei|ja|分霊}}) of Hiyoshi Taisha. These shrines primarily worship {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主}} (or {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}), totaling about 3,800 nationwide.
During the {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} period, it was also known as '{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}}' or 'Hiyoshi Sannō,' and is affectionately called '''Sannō-san''' even today. Although Hiyoshi Taisha uses monkeys ({{ill|saru|ja|サル}}) as divine messengers, the exact relation with monkeys is not well understood and may reflect remnants of primitive worship.
== History == === Establishment of Hiyoshi Taisha and Enryaku-ji === Hiyoshi Taisha originally enshrined {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神}}, deity of Mount Hie (later named [[Mount Hiei]]). After the relocation of the capital to {{ill|Ōmi-kyō|ja|近江京}}, in the seventh year of Emperor Tenji ([[668]]), {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kami|ja|大三輪神}} (Ōmononushi no Kami) was enshrined from Mount Miwa (Yamato Province) to protect the new capital.
When the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗}} monastery of [[Enryaku-ji]] was founded on Mount Hiei, these deities became the local guardian deities. Saichō, founder of Enryaku-ji, set these local deities as the 'Various Sannō' of Mount Hiei.<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō of Hiyoshi Taisha] (Tsutomu Egashira, Iwakura Society Bulletin No. 28, July 12, 2013)</ref> The term "Sannō" was adopted following the deity "Sannō Hitsu Shinkun" enshrined at the {{ill|Tiantai Mountain|ja|天台山}} Guoqing Temple in Tang China.<ref name="iwakura" />
Saichō referred to "Sannō" as a Buddhist expression of local mountain deities, avoiding explicitly calling them "kami".<ref name="iwakura" /> This approach laid the foundation for the Shinbutsu-shūgō (syncretic) "Sannō Shintō".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Ryōsho Sansei === In [[825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍}} introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch {{ill|Ryōgen|ja|良源}} elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
== Hiyoshi/Hie Shrines Nationwide == {{Main|Sannō Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine|Hie Shrine}} === Head Shrine === * [[Hiyoshi Taisha]]
=== Notable Shrines === * [[Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|Hie Shrine]] (Tokyo) * [[Hie Shrine (Toyama)|Hie Shrine]] (Toyama)

After the relocation of the capital to {{ill|Ōmi-kyō|ja|近江京|12=draft}}, in the seventh year of Emperor Tenji ([[668]]), {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kami|ja|大三輪神|12=draft}} (Ōmononushi no Kami) was enshrined from Mount Miwa (Yamato Province) to protect the new capital.
When the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft}} monastery of [[Enryaku-ji]] was founded on Mount Hiei, these deities became the local guardian deities. Saichō, founder of Enryaku-ji, set these local deities as the 'Various Sannō' of Mount Hiei.<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō of Hiyoshi Taisha] (Tsutomu Egashira, Iwakura Society Bulletin No. 28, July 12, 2013)</ref> The term "Sannō" was adopted following the deity "Sannō Hitsu Shinkun" enshrined at the {{ill|Tiantai Mountain|ja|天台山|12=draft}} Guoqing Temple in Tang China.<ref name="iwakura" />
Saichō referred to "Sannō" as a Buddhist expression of local mountain deities, avoiding explicitly calling them "kami".<ref name="iwakura" /> This approach laid the foundation for the Shinbutsu-shūgō (syncretic) "Sannō Shintō".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Ryōsho Sansei === In [[825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄|12=draft}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍|12=draft}} introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch {{ill|Ryōgen|ja|良源|12=draft}} elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.

=== Notable Shrines === * [[Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|Hie Shrine]] (Tokyo) * [[Hie Shrine (Toyama)|Hie Shrine]] (Toyama)

{{One source|date=June 2023}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha nishihongu-honden.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine]] '''Sannō Faith''' (山王信仰) is a [[Shinto]] belief originating from the [[Hiyoshi Taisha]] located at the foot of [[Mount Hiei]] ([[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu City]], [[Shiga Prefecture]]).
== Terminology == Sannō refers to another name for the deity worshipped at Hiyoshi Taisha in [[Sakamoto, Shiga|Sakamoto]], Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture, specifically indicating the deity enshrined on Mount Hiei.
Shrines named '''[[Hiyoshi Shrine]]''', '''[[Hie Shrine]]''', or '''Sannō Shrine''' across Japan are derived from Sannō Faith, and they enshrine the divided spirits ({{ill|Bunrei|ja|分霊|12=draft|12=draft}}) of Hiyoshi Taisha. These shrines primarily worship {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主|12=draft|12=draft}} (or {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft|12=draft}}), totaling about 3,800 nationwide.
During the {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|12=draft|12=draft}} period, it was also known as '{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=draft|12=draft}}' or 'Hiyoshi Sannō,' and is affectionately called '''Sannō-san''' even today. Although Hiyoshi Taisha uses monkeys ({{ill|saru|ja|サル|12=draft|12=draft}}) as divine messengers, the exact relation with monkeys is not well understood and may reflect remnants of primitive worship.
== History == === Establishment of Hiyoshi Taisha and Enryaku-ji === Hiyoshi Taisha originally enshrined {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神|12=draft|12=draft}}, deity of Mount Hie (later named [[Mount Hiei]]). After the relocation of the capital to {{ill|Ōmi-kyō|ja|近江京|12=draft|12=draft}}, in the seventh year of Emperor Tenji ([[668]]), {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kami|ja|大三輪神|12=draft|12=draft}} (Ōmononushi no Kami) was enshrined from Mount Miwa (Yamato Province) to protect the new capital.
When the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft}} monastery of [[Enryaku-ji]] was founded on Mount Hiei, these deities became the local guardian deities. Saichō, founder of Enryaku-ji, set these local deities as the 'Various Sannō' of Mount Hiei.<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō of Hiyoshi Taisha] (Tsutomu Egashira, Iwakura Society Bulletin No. 28, July 12, 2013)</ref> The term "Sannō" was adopted following the deity "Sannō Hitsu Shinkun" enshrined at the {{ill|Tiantai Mountain|ja|天台山|12=draft|12=draft}} Guoqing Temple in Tang China.<ref name="iwakura" />
Saichō referred to "Sannō" as a Buddhist expression of local mountain deities, avoiding explicitly calling them "kami".<ref name="iwakura" /> This approach laid the foundation for the Shinbutsu-shūgō (syncretic) "Sannō Shintō".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Ryōsho Sansei === In [[825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄|12=draft|12=draft}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍|12=draft|12=draft}} introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch {{ill|Ryōgen|ja|良源|12=draft|12=draft}} elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.

[[ja:山王信仰]]
{{citation needed|Sannō Faith}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{One source|date=June 2023}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha nishihongu-honden.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine]] '''Sannō Faith''' (山王信仰) is a [[Shinto]] belief originating from the [[Hiyoshi Taisha]] located at the foot of [[Mount Hiei]] ([[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu City]], [[Shiga Prefecture]]).
== Terminology == Sannō refers to another name for the deity worshipped at Hiyoshi Taisha in [[Sakamoto, Shiga|Sakamoto]], Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture, specifically indicating the deity enshrined on Mount Hiei.
Shrines named '''[[Hiyoshi Shrine]]''', '''[[Hie Shrine]]''', or '''Sannō Shrine''' across Japan are derived from Sannō Faith, and they enshrine the divided spirits ({{ill|Bunrei|ja|分霊|12=draft|12=draft}}) of Hiyoshi Taisha. These shrines primarily worship {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主|12=draft|12=draft}} (or {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft|12=draft}}), totaling about 3,800 nationwide.
During the {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|12=draft|12=draft}} period, it was also known as '{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=draft|12=draft}}' or 'Hiyoshi Sannō,' and is affectionately called '''Sannō-san''' even today. Although Hiyoshi Taisha uses monkeys ({{ill|saru|ja|サル|12=draft|12=draft}}) as divine messengers, the exact relation with monkeys is not well understood and may reflect remnants of primitive worship.
== History == === Establishment of Hiyoshi Taisha and Enryaku-ji === Hiyoshi Taisha originally enshrined {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神|12=draft|12=draft}}, deity of Mount Hie (later named [[Mount Hiei]]). After the relocation of the capital to {{ill|Ōmi-kyō|ja|近江京|12=draft|12=draft}}, in the seventh year of Emperor Tenji ([[668]]), {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kami|ja|大三輪神|12=draft|12=draft}} (Ōmononushi no Kami) was enshrined from Mount Miwa (Yamato Province) to protect the new capital.
When the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft}} monastery of [[Enryaku-ji]] was founded on Mount Hiei, these deities became the local guardian deities. Saichō, founder of Enryaku-ji, set these local deities as the 'Various Sannō' of Mount Hiei.<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō of Hiyoshi Taisha] (Tsutomu Egashira, Iwakura Society Bulletin No. 28, July 12, 2013)</ref> The term "Sannō" was adopted following the deity "Sannō Hitsu Shinkun" enshrined at the {{ill|Tiantai Mountain|ja|天台山|12=draft|12=draft}} Guoqing Temple in Tang China.<ref name="iwakura" />
Saichō referred to "Sannō" as a Buddhist expression of local mountain deities, avoiding explicitly calling them "kami".<ref name="iwakura" /> This approach laid the foundation for the Shinbutsu-shūgō (syncretic) "Sannō Shintō".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Ryōsho Sansei === In [[825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄|12=draft|12=draft}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍|12=draft|12=draft}} introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch {{ill|Ryōgen|ja|良源|12=draft|12=draft}} elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
== Hiyoshi/Hie Shrines Nationwide == {{Main|Sannō Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine|Hie Shrine}} === Head Shrine === * [[Hiyoshi Taisha]]
=== Notable Shrines === * [[Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|Hie Shrine]] (Tokyo) * [[Hie Shrine (Toyama)|Hie Shrine]] (Toyama)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == *{{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html |title=Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō (Tsutomu Egashira)|date=20140714221329}} == See Also == *[[Sannō Shintō]] *[[Hiyoshi Taisha]] *[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]

{{One source|date=June 2023}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha nishihongu-honden.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine]] '''Sannō Faith''' (山王信仰) is a [[Shinto]] belief originating from the [[Hiyoshi Taisha]] located at the foot of [[Mount Hiei]] ([[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu City]], [[Shiga Prefecture]]).
== Terminology == Sannō refers to another name for the deity worshipped at Hiyoshi Taisha in [[Sakamoto, Shiga|Sakamoto]], Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture, specifically indicating the deity enshrined on Mount Hiei.
Shrines named '''[[Hiyoshi Shrine]]''', '''[[Hie Shrine]]''', or '''Sannō Shrine''' across Japan are derived from Sannō Faith, and they enshrine the divided spirits ({{ill|Bunrei|ja|分霊|12=draft|12=draft}}) of Hiyoshi Taisha. These shrines primarily worship {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主|12=draft|12=draft}} (or {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft|12=draft}}), totaling about 3,800 nationwide.
During the {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|12=draft|12=draft}} period, it was also known as '{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=draft|12=draft}}' or 'Hiyoshi Sannō,' and is affectionately called '''Sannō-san''' even today. Although Hiyoshi Taisha uses [[monkeys]] as divine messengers, the exact relation with monkeys is not well understood and may reflect remnants of primitive worship.
== History == === Establishment of Hiyoshi Taisha and Enryaku-ji === Hiyoshi Taisha originally enshrined {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神|12=draft|12=draft}}, deity of Mount Hie (later named [[Mount Hiei]]). === Ryōsho Sansei === In [[825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄|12=draft|12=draft}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍|12=draft|12=draft}} introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />

This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />

;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine

=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch {{ill|Ryōgen|ja|良源|12=draft|12=draft}} elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
== Hiyoshi/Hie Shrines Nationwide == {{Main|Sannō Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine|Hie Shrine}} === Head Shrine === * [[Hiyoshi Taisha]]
=== Notable Shrines === * [[Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|Hie Shrine]] (Tokyo) * [[Hie Shrine (Toyama)|Hie Shrine]] (Toyama)

revision.text:
{{One source|date=June 2023}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha nishihongu-honden.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine]] '''Sannō Faith''' (山王信仰) is a [[Shinto]] belief originating from the [[Hiyoshi Taisha]] located at the foot of [[Mount Hiei]] ([[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu City]], [[Shiga Prefecture]]).
== Terminology == Sannō refers to another name for the deity worshipped at Hiyoshi Taisha in [[Sakamoto, Shiga|Sakamoto]], Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture, specifically indicating the deity enshrined on Mount Hiei.
Shrines named '''[[Hiyoshi Shrine]]''', '''[[Hie Shrine]]''', or '''Sannō Shrine''' across Japan are derived from Sannō Faith, and they enshrine the divided spirits ({{ill|Bunrei|ja|分霊|12=draft|12=draft}}) of Hiyoshi Taisha. These shrines primarily worship {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主|12=draft|12=draft}} (or {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft|12=draft}}), totaling about 3,800 nationwide.
During the {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|12=draft|12=draft}} period, it was also known as '{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=draft|12=draft}}' or 'Hiyoshi Sannō,' and is affectionately called '''Sannō-san''' even today. Although Hiyoshi Taisha uses [[monkeys]] as divine messengers, the exact relation with monkeys is not well understood and may reflect remnants of primitive worship.
== History == === Establishment of Hiyoshi Taisha and Enryaku-ji === Hiyoshi Taisha originally enshrined {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神|12=draft|12=draft}}, deity of Mount Hie (later named [[Mount Hiei]]). After the relocation of the capital to {{ill|Ōmi-kyō|ja|近江京|12=draft|12=draft}}, in the seventh year of Emperor Tenji ([[668]]), {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kami|ja|大三輪神|1=Ōmononushi|2=ja|3=大物主|lt=Ōmiwa no Kami|4=ru|5=Омононуси|6=fr|7=Ōmononushi|12=draft|12=draft}} (Ōmononushi no Kami) was enshrined from Mount Miwa (Yamato Province) to protect the new capital.
When the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|12=draft|12=draft}} monastery of [[Enryaku-ji]] was founded on Mount Hiei, these deities became the local guardian deities. Saichō, founder of Enryaku-ji, set these local deities as the 'Various Sannō' of Mount Hiei.<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō of Hiyoshi Taisha] (Tsutomu Egashira, Iwakura Society Bulletin No. 28, July 12, 2013)</ref> The term "Sannō" was adopted following the deity "Sannō Hitsu Shinkun" enshrined at the {{ill|Tiantai Mountain|ja|天台山|12=draft|12=draft}} Guoqing Temple in Tang China.<ref name="iwakura" />
Saichō referred to "Sannō" as a Buddhist expression of local mountain deities, avoiding explicitly calling them "kami".<ref name="iwakura" /> This approach laid the foundation for the Shinbutsu-shūgō (syncretic) "Sannō Shintō".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Ryōsho Sansei === In [[825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄|12=draft|12=draft}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍|12=draft|12=draft}} introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch {{ill|Ryōgen|ja|良源|12=draft|12=draft}} elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
== Hiyoshi/Hie Shrines Nationwide == {{Main|Sannō Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine|Hie Shrine}} === Head Shrine === * [[Hiyoshi Taisha]]
=== Notable Shrines === * [[Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|Hie Shrine]] (Tokyo) * [[Hie Shrine (Toyama)|Hie Shrine]] (Toyama)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == *{{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html |title=Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō (Tsutomu Egashira)|date=20140714221329}} == See Also == *[[Sannō Shintō]] *[[Hiyoshi Taisha]] *[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]

revision.text:
{{One source|date=June 2023}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha nishihongu-honden.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine]] '''Sannō Faith''' (山王信仰) is a [[Shinto]] belief originating from the [[Hiyoshi Taisha]] located at the foot of [[Mount Hiei]] ([[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu City]], [[Shiga Prefecture]]).
== Terminology == Sannō refers to another name for the deity worshipped at Hiyoshi Taisha in [[Sakamoto, Shiga|Sakamoto]], Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture, specifically indicating the deity enshrined on Mount Hiei.
Shrines named '''[[Hiyoshi Shrine]]''', '''[[Hie Shrine]]''', or '''Sannō Shrine''' across Japan are derived from Sannō Faith, and they enshrine the divided spirits ({{ill|Bunrei|ja|分霊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) of Hiyoshi Taisha. These shrines primarily worship {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (or {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), totaling about 3,800 nationwide.
During the {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|qq=draft|qq=draft}} period, it was also known as '{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|qq=draft|qq=draft}}' or 'Hiyoshi Sannō,' and is affectionately called '''Sannō-san''' even today. Although Hiyoshi Taisha uses [[monkeys]] as divine messengers, the exact relation with monkeys is not well understood and may reflect remnants of primitive worship.
== History == === Establishment of Hiyoshi Taisha and Enryaku-ji === Hiyoshi Taisha originally enshrined {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, deity of Mount Hie (later named [[Mount Hiei]]). After the relocation of the capital to {{ill|Ōmi-kyō|ja|近江京|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, in the seventh year of Emperor Tenji ([[668]]), {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kami|ja|大三輪神|1=Ōmononushi|2=ja|3=大物主|lt=Ōmiwa no Kami|4=ru|5=Омононуси|6=fr|7=Ōmononushi|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Ōmononushi no Kami) was enshrined from Mount Miwa (Yamato Province) to protect the new capital.
When the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|qq=draft|qq=draft}} monastery of [[Enryaku-ji]] was founded on Mount Hiei, these deities became the local guardian deities. Saichō, founder of Enryaku-ji, set these local deities as the 'Various Sannō' of Mount Hiei.<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō of Hiyoshi Taisha] (Tsutomu Egashira, Iwakura Society Bulletin No. 28, July 12, 2013)</ref> The term "Sannō" was adopted following the deity "Sannō Hitsu Shinkun" enshrined at the {{ill|Tiantai Mountain|ja|天台山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Guoqing Temple in Tang China.<ref name="iwakura" />
Saichō referred to "Sannō" as a Buddhist expression of local mountain deities, avoiding explicitly calling them "kami".<ref name="iwakura" /> This approach laid the foundation for the Shinbutsu-shūgō (syncretic) "Sannō Shintō".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Ryōsho Sansei === In [[825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄|qq=draft|qq=draft}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍|qq=draft|qq=draft}} introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch {{ill|Ryōgen|ja|良源|qq=draft|qq=draft}} elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
== Hiyoshi/Hie Shrines Nationwide == {{Main|Sannō Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine|Hie Shrine}} === Head Shrine === * [[Hiyoshi Taisha]]
=== Notable Shrines === * [[Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|Hie Shrine]] (Tokyo) * [[Hie Shrine (Toyama)|Hie Shrine]] (Toyama)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == *{{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html |title=Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō (Tsutomu Egashira)|date=20140714221329}} == See Also == *[[Sannō Shintō]] *[[Hiyoshi Taisha]] *[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]
{{draft categories|
}}

Later, fifth patriarch {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍|qq=draft|qq=draft}} introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch {{ill|Ryōgen|ja|良源|qq=draft|qq=draft}} elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
== Hiyoshi/Hie Shrines Nationwide == {{Main|Sannō Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine|Hie Shrine}} === Head Shrine === * [[Hiyoshi Taisha]]
=== Notable Shrines === * [[Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|Hie Shrine]] (Tokyo) * [[Hie Shrine (Toyama)|Hie Shrine]] (Toyama)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == *{{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html |title=Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō (Tsutomu Egashira)|date=20140714221329}} == See Also == *[[Sannō Shintō]] *[[Hiyoshi Taisha]] *[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]

revision.text:
{{One source|date=June 2023}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha nishihongu-honden.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine]] '''Sannō Faith''' (山王信仰) is a [[Shinto]] belief originating from the [[Hiyoshi Taisha]] located at the foot of [[Mount Hiei]] ([[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu City]], [[Shiga Prefecture]]).
== Terminology == Sannō refers to another name for the deity worshipped at Hiyoshi Taisha in [[Sakamoto, Shiga|Sakamoto]], Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture, specifically indicating the deity enshrined on Mount Hiei.
Shrines named '''[[Hiyoshi Shrine]]''', '''[[Hie Shrine]]''', or '''Sannō Shrine''' across Japan are derived from Sannō Faith, and they enshrine the divided spirits ({{ill|Bunrei|ja|分霊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) of Hiyoshi Taisha. These shrines primarily worship {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Ōmononushi|ja|大物主|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (or {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), totaling about 3,800 nationwide.
During the {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|qq=draft|qq=draft}} period, it was also known as '{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|qq=draft|qq=draft}}' or 'Hiyoshi Sannō,' and is affectionately called '''Sannō-san''' even today. Although Hiyoshi Taisha uses [[monkeys]] as divine messengers, the exact relation with monkeys is not well understood and may reflect remnants of primitive worship.
== History == === Establishment of Hiyoshi Taisha and Enryaku-ji === Hiyoshi Taisha originally enshrined {{ill|Ōyamakui no Kami|ja|大山咋神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, deity of Mount Hie (later named [[Mount Hiei]]). After the relocation of the capital to {{ill|Ōmi-kyō|ja|近江京|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, in the seventh year of Emperor Tenji ([[668]]), {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kami|ja|大三輪神|1=Ōmononushi|2=ja|3=大物主|lt=Ōmiwa no Kami|4=ru|5=Омононуси|6=fr|7=Ōmononushi|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Ōmononushi no Kami) was enshrined from Mount Miwa (Yamato Province) to protect the new capital.
When the {{ill|Tendai|ja|天台宗|qq=draft|qq=draft}} monastery of [[Enryaku-ji]] was founded on Mount Hiei, these deities became the local guardian deities. Saichō, founder of Enryaku-ji, set these local deities as the 'Various Sannō' of Mount Hiei.<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō of Hiyoshi Taisha] (Tsutomu Egashira, Iwakura Society Bulletin No. 28, July 12, 2013)</ref> The term "Sannō" was adopted following the deity "Sannō Hitsu Shinkun" enshrined at the {{ill|Tiantai Mountain|ja|天台山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Guoqing Temple in Tang China.<ref name="iwakura" />
Saichō referred to "Sannō" as a Buddhist expression of local mountain deities, avoiding explicitly calling them "kami".<ref name="iwakura" /> This approach laid the foundation for the Shinbutsu-shūgō (syncretic) "Sannō Shintō".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Ryōsho Sansei === In [[825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄|qq=draft|qq=draft}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch {{ill|Enchin|ja|円珍|qq=draft|qq=draft}} introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch {{ill|Ryōgen|ja|良源|qq=draft|qq=draft}} elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
== Hiyoshi/Hie Shrines Nationwide == {{Main|Sannō Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine|Hie Shrine}} === Head Shrine === * [[Hiyoshi Taisha]]
=== Notable Shrines === * [[Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|Hie Shrine]] (Tokyo) * [[Hie Shrine (Toyama)|Hie Shrine]] (Toyama)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == *{{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html |title=Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō (Tsutomu Egashira)|date=20140714221329}} == See Also == *[[Sannō Shintō]] *[[Hiyoshi Taisha]] *[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]

;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.

revision.comment: Bot: replace {{ill}} with enwiki link via Wikidata
revision.text:
{{One source|date=June 2023}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha nishihongu-honden.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine]] '''Sannō Faith''' (山王信仰) is a [[Shinto]] belief originating from the [[Hiyoshi Taisha]] located at the foot of [[Mount Hiei]] ([[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu City]], [[Shiga Prefecture]]).
== Terminology == Sannō refers to another name for the deity worshipped at Hiyoshi Taisha in [[Sakamoto, Shiga|Sakamoto]], Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture, specifically indicating the deity enshrined on Mount Hiei.
Shrines named '''[[Hiyoshi Shrine]]''', '''[[Hie Shrine]]''', or '''Sannō Shrine''' across Japan are derived from Sannō Faith, and they enshrine the divided spirits ([[:en:Bunrei|Bunrei]]) of Hiyoshi Taisha. These shrines primarily worship [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]] and [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] (or [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]), totaling about 3,800 nationwide.
During the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] period, it was also known as '{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|qq=}}' or 'Hiyoshi Sannō,' and is affectionately called '''Sannō-san''' even today. Although Hiyoshi Taisha uses [[monkeys]] as divine messengers, the exact relation with monkeys is not well understood and may reflect remnants of primitive worship.
== History == === Establishment of Hiyoshi Taisha and Enryaku-ji === Hiyoshi Taisha originally enshrined [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]], deity of Mount Hie (later named [[Mount Hiei]]). After the relocation of the capital to {{ill|Ōmi-kyō|ja|近江京|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-kyō|qq=}}, in the seventh year of Emperor Tenji ([[668]]), {{ill|Ōmiwa no Kami|ja|大三輪神|1=Ōmononushi|2=ja|3=大物主|lt=Ōmiwa no Kami|4=ru|5=Омононуси|6=fr|7=Ōmononushi|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=}} (Ōmononushi no Kami) was enshrined from Mount Miwa (Yamato Province) to protect the new capital.
When the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monastery of [[Enryaku-ji]] was founded on Mount Hiei, these deities became the local guardian deities. Saichō, founder of Enryaku-ji, set these local deities as the 'Various Sannō' of Mount Hiei.<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō of Hiyoshi Taisha] (Tsutomu Egashira, Iwakura Society Bulletin No. 28, July 12, 2013)</ref> The term "Sannō" was adopted following the deity "Sannō Hitsu Shinkun" enshrined at the [[:en:Tiantai Mountain|Tiantai Mountain]] Guoqing Temple in Tang China.<ref name="iwakura" />
Saichō referred to "Sannō" as a Buddhist expression of local mountain deities, avoiding explicitly calling them "kami".<ref name="iwakura" /> This approach laid the foundation for the Shinbutsu-shūgō (syncretic) "Sannō Shintō".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Ryōsho Sansei === In [[825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchō|qq=}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch [[:en:Enchin|Enchin]] introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch [[:en:Ryōgen|Ryōgen]] elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
== Hiyoshi/Hie Shrines Nationwide == {{Main|Sannō Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine|Hie Shrine}} === Head Shrine === * [[Hiyoshi Taisha]]
=== Notable Shrines === * [[Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|Hie Shrine]] (Tokyo) * [[Hie Shrine (Toyama)|Hie Shrine]] (Toyama)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == *{{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html |title=Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō (Tsutomu Egashira)|date=20140714221329}} == See Also == *[[Sannō Shintō]] *[[Hiyoshi Taisha]] *[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]
{{translated page|ja|山王信仰|version=101692845|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Saichō, founder of Enryaku-ji, set these local deities as the 'Various Sannō' of Mount Hiei.<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō of Hiyoshi Taisha] (Tsutomu Egashira, Iwakura Society Bulletin No. 28, July 12, 2013)</ref> The term "Sannō" was adopted following the deity "Sannō Hitsu Shinkun" enshrined at the [[:en:Tiantai Mountain|Tiantai Mountain]] Guoqing Temple in Tang China.<ref name="iwakura" />
Saichō referred to "Sannō" as a Buddhist expression of local mountain deities, avoiding explicitly calling them "kami".<ref name="iwakura" /> This approach laid the foundation for the Shinbutsu-shūgō (syncretic) "Sannō Shintō".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Ryōsho Sansei === In [[825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchō|qq=}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch [[:en:Enchin|Enchin]] introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch [[:en:Ryōgen|Ryōgen]] elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
== Hiyoshi/Hie Shrines Nationwide == {{Main|Sannō Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine|Hie Shrine}} === Head Shrine === * [[Hiyoshi Taisha]]
=== Notable Shrines === * [[Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|Hie Shrine]] (Tokyo) * [[Hie Shrine (Toyama)|Hie Shrine]] (Toyama)

== Bibliography == *{{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html |title=Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō (Tsutomu Egashira)|date=20140714221329}} == See Also == *[[Sannō Shintō]] *[[Hiyoshi Taisha]] *[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]
