revision.text:
{{One source|date=June 2023}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha nishihongu-honden.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine]] '''Sannō Faith''' (山王信仰) is a [[Shinto]] belief originating from the [[Hiyoshi Taisha]] located at the foot of [[Mount Hiei]] ([[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu City]], [[Shiga Prefecture]]).
== Terminology == Sannō refers to another name for the deity worshipped at Hiyoshi Taisha in [[Sakamoto, Shiga|Sakamoto]], Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture, specifically indicating the deity enshrined on Mount Hiei.
Shrines named '''[[Hiyoshi Shrine]]''', '''[[Hie Shrine]]''', or '''Sannō Shrine''' across Japan are derived from Sannō Faith, and they enshrine the divided spirits ([[:en:Bunrei|Bunrei]]) of Hiyoshi Taisha. These shrines primarily worship [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]] and [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] (or [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]), totaling about 3,800 nationwide.
During the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] period, it was also known as '{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}' or 'Hiyoshi Sannō,' and is affectionately called '''Sannō-san''' even today. Although Hiyoshi Taisha uses [[monkeys]] as divine messengers, the exact relation with monkeys is not well understood and may reflect remnants of primitive worship.
== History == === Establishment of Hiyoshi Taisha and Enryaku-ji === Hiyoshi Taisha originally enshrined [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]], deity of Mount Hie (later named [[Mount Hiei]]). After the relocation of the capital to {{ill|Ōmi-kyō|ja|近江京|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-kyō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近江大津宮}}, in the seventh year of Emperor Tenji ([[668]]), [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmiwa no Kami]] (Ōmononushi no Kami) was enshrined from Mount Miwa (Yamato Province) to protect the new capital.
When the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monastery of [[Enryaku-ji]] was founded on Mount Hiei, these deities became the local guardian deities. Saichō, founder of Enryaku-ji, set these local deities as the 'Various Sannō' of Mount Hiei.<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō of Hiyoshi Taisha] (Tsutomu Egashira, Iwakura Society Bulletin No. 28, July 12, 2013)</ref> The term "Sannō" was adopted following the deity "Sannō Hitsu Shinkun" enshrined at the [[:en:Tiantai Mountain|Tiantai Mountain]] Guoqing Temple in Tang China.<ref name="iwakura" />
Saichō referred to "Sannō" as a Buddhist expression of local mountain deities, avoiding explicitly calling them "kami".<ref name="iwakura" /> This approach laid the foundation for the Shinbutsu-shūgō (syncretic) "Sannō Shintō".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Ryōsho Sansei === In [[825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchō|qq=}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch [[:en:Enchin|Enchin]] introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch [[:en:Ryōgen|Ryōgen]] elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{One source|date=June 2023}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha nishihongu-honden.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine]] '''Sannō Faith''' (山王信仰) is a [[Shinto]] belief originating from the [[Hiyoshi Taisha]] located at the foot of [[Mount Hiei]] ([[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu City]], [[Shiga Prefecture]]).
== Terminology == Sannō refers to another name for the deity worshipped at Hiyoshi Taisha in [[Sakamoto, Shiga|Sakamoto]], Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture, specifically indicating the deity enshrined on Mount Hiei.
Shrines named '''[[Hiyoshi Shrine]]''', '''[[Hie Shrine]]''', or '''Sannō Shrine''' across Japan are derived from Sannō Faith, and they enshrine the divided spirits ([[:en:Bunrei|Bunrei]]) of Hiyoshi Taisha. These shrines primarily worship [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]] and [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] (or [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]), totaling about 3,800 nationwide.
During the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] period, it was also known as '{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}' or 'Hiyoshi Sannō,' and is affectionately called '''Sannō-san''' even today. Although Hiyoshi Taisha uses [[monkeys]] as divine messengers, the exact relation with monkeys is not well understood and may reflect remnants of primitive worship.
== History == === Establishment of Hiyoshi Taisha and Enryaku-ji === Hiyoshi Taisha originally enshrined [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]], deity of Mount Hie (later named [[Mount Hiei]]). After the relocation of the capital to {{ill|Ōmi-kyō|ja|近江京|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-kyō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近江大津宮}}, in the seventh year of Emperor Tenji ([[:en:668|668]]), [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmiwa no Kami]] (Ōmononushi no Kami) was enshrined from Mount Miwa (Yamato Province) to protect the new capital.
When the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monastery of [[Enryaku-ji]] was founded on Mount Hiei, these deities became the local guardian deities. Saichō, founder of Enryaku-ji, set these local deities as the 'Various Sannō' of Mount Hiei.<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō of Hiyoshi Taisha] (Tsutomu Egashira, Iwakura Society Bulletin No. 28, July 12, 2013)</ref> The term "Sannō" was adopted following the deity "Sannō Hitsu Shinkun" enshrined at the [[:en:Tiantai Mountain|Tiantai Mountain]] Guoqing Temple in Tang China.<ref name="iwakura" />
Saichō referred to "Sannō" as a Buddhist expression of local mountain deities, avoiding explicitly calling them "kami".<ref name="iwakura" /> This approach laid the foundation for the Shinbutsu-shūgō (syncretic) "Sannō Shintō".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Ryōsho Sansei === In [[:en:825|825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchō|qq=}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch [[:en:Enchin|Enchin]] introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch [[:en:Ryōgen|Ryōgen]] elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[:en:1571|1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
== Hiyoshi/Hie Shrines Nationwide == {{Main|Sannō Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine|Hie Shrine}} === Head Shrine === * [[Hiyoshi Taisha]]
=== Notable Shrines === * [[Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|Hie Shrine]] (Tokyo) * [[Hie Shrine (Toyama)|Hie Shrine]] (Toyama)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == *{{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html |title=Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō (Tsutomu Egashira)|date=20140714221329}} == See Also == *[[Sannō Shintō]] *[[Hiyoshi Taisha]] *[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]
{{translated page|ja|山王信仰|version=101692845|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Later, fifth patriarch [[:en:Enchin|Enchin]] introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch [[:en:Ryōgen|Ryōgen]] elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[:en:972|972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[:en:993|993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[:en:1571|1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
== Hiyoshi/Hie Shrines Nationwide == {{Main|Sannō Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine|Hie Shrine}} === Head Shrine === * [[Hiyoshi Taisha]]
=== Notable Shrines === * [[Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|Hie Shrine]] (Tokyo) * [[Hie Shrine (Toyama)|Hie Shrine]] (Toyama)

revision.text:
{{One source|date=June 2023}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha nishihongu-honden.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine]] '''Sannō Faith''' (山王信仰) is a [[Shinto]] belief originating from the [[Hiyoshi Taisha]] located at the foot of [[Mount Hiei]] ([[:en:Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu City]], [[Shiga Prefecture]]).
== Terminology == Sannō refers to another name for the deity worshipped at Hiyoshi Taisha in [[Sakamoto, Shiga|Sakamoto]], Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture, specifically indicating the deity enshrined on Mount Hiei.
Shrines named '''[[Hiyoshi Shrine]]''', '''[[Hie Shrine]]''', or '''Sannō Shrine''' across Japan are derived from Sannō Faith, and they enshrine the divided spirits ([[:en:Bunrei|Bunrei]]) of Hiyoshi Taisha. These shrines primarily worship [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]] and [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] (or [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]), totaling about 3,800 nationwide.
During the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] period, it was also known as '{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}' or 'Hiyoshi Sannō,' and is affectionately called '''Sannō-san''' even today. Although Hiyoshi Taisha uses [[monkeys]] as divine messengers, the exact relation with monkeys is not well understood and may reflect remnants of primitive worship.
== History == === Establishment of Hiyoshi Taisha and Enryaku-ji === Hiyoshi Taisha originally enshrined [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]], deity of Mount Hie (later named [[Mount Hiei]]). After the relocation of the capital to {{ill|Ōmi-kyō|ja|近江京|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-kyō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近江大津宮}}, in the seventh year of Emperor Tenji ([[:en:668|668]]), [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmiwa no Kami]] (Ōmononushi no Kami) was enshrined from Mount Miwa (Yamato Province) to protect the new capital.
When the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monastery of [[Enryaku-ji]] was founded on Mount Hiei, these deities became the local guardian deities. Saichō, founder of Enryaku-ji, set these local deities as the 'Various Sannō' of Mount Hiei.<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō of Hiyoshi Taisha] (Tsutomu Egashira, Iwakura Society Bulletin No. 28, July 12, 2013)</ref> The term "Sannō" was adopted following the deity "Sannō Hitsu Shinkun" enshrined at the [[:en:Tiantai Mountain|Tiantai Mountain]] Guoqing Temple in Tang China.<ref name="iwakura" />
Saichō referred to "Sannō" as a Buddhist expression of local mountain deities, avoiding explicitly calling them "kami".<ref name="iwakura" /> This approach laid the foundation for the Shinbutsu-shūgō (syncretic) "Sannō Shintō".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Ryōsho Sansei === In [[:en:825|825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchō|qq=}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch [[:en:Enchin|Enchin]] introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch [[:en:Ryōgen|Ryōgen]] elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[:en:972|972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[:en:993|993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[:en:1571|1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
== Hiyoshi/Hie Shrines Nationwide == {{Main|Sannō Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine|Hie Shrine}} === Head Shrine === * [[Hiyoshi Taisha]]
=== Notable Shrines === * [[Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|Hie Shrine]] (Tokyo) * [[Hie Shrine (Toyama)|Hie Shrine]] (Toyama)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == *{{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html |title=Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō (Tsutomu Egashira)|date=20140714221329}} == See Also == *[[Sannō Shintō]] *[[Hiyoshi Taisha]] *[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]

{{One source|date=June 2023}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha nishihongu-honden.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine]] '''Sannō Faith''' (山王信仰) is a [[Shinto]] belief originating from the [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] located at the foot of [[Mount Hiei]] ([[:en:Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu City]], [[Shiga Prefecture]]).
== Terminology == Sannō refers to another name for the deity worshipped at Hiyoshi Taisha in [[Sakamoto, Shiga|Sakamoto]], Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture, specifically indicating the deity enshrined on Mount Hiei.
Shrines named '''[[:en:Hiyoshi Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine]]''', '''[[Hie Shrine]]''', or '''Sannō Shrine''' across Japan are derived from Sannō Faith, and they enshrine the divided spirits ([[:en:Bunrei|Bunrei]]) of Hiyoshi Taisha. These shrines primarily worship [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]] and [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] (or [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]), totaling about 3,800 nationwide.
During the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] period, it was also known as '{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}' or 'Hiyoshi Sannō,' and is affectionately called '''Sannō-san''' even today. Although Hiyoshi Taisha uses [[:en:monkeys|monkeys]] as divine messengers, the exact relation with monkeys is not well understood and may reflect remnants of primitive worship.
== History == === Establishment of Hiyoshi Taisha and Enryaku-ji === Hiyoshi Taisha originally enshrined [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]], deity of Mount Hie (later named [[Mount Hiei]]). After the relocation of the capital to {{ill|Ōmi-kyō|ja|近江京|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-kyō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近江大津宮}}, in the seventh year of Emperor Tenji ([[:en:668|668]]), [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmiwa no Kami]] (Ōmononushi no Kami) was enshrined from Mount Miwa (Yamato Province) to protect the new capital.
When the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monastery of [[Enryaku-ji]] was founded on Mount Hiei, these deities became the local guardian deities. Saichō, founder of Enryaku-ji, set these local deities as the 'Various Sannō' of Mount Hiei.<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō of Hiyoshi Taisha] (Tsutomu Egashira, Iwakura Society Bulletin No. 28, July 12, 2013)</ref> The term "Sannō" was adopted following the deity "Sannō Hitsu Shinkun" enshrined at the [[:en:Tiantai Mountain|Tiantai Mountain]] Guoqing Temple in Tang China.<ref name="iwakura" />
Saichō referred to "Sannō" as a Buddhist expression of local mountain deities, avoiding explicitly calling them "kami".<ref name="iwakura" /> This approach laid the foundation for the Shinbutsu-shūgō (syncretic) "Sannō Shintō".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Ryōsho Sansei === In [[:en:825|825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchō|qq=}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch [[:en:Enchin|Enchin]] introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch [[:en:Ryōgen|Ryōgen]] elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[:en:972|972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[:en:993|993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[:en:1571|1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
== Hiyoshi/Hie Shrines Nationwide == {{Main|Sannō Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine|Hie Shrine}} === Head Shrine === * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]]
=== Notable Shrines === * [[Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|Hie Shrine]] (Tokyo) * [[Hie Shrine (Toyama)|Hie Shrine]] (Toyama)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == *{{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html |title=Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō (Tsutomu Egashira)|date=20140714221329}} == See Also == *[[Sannō Shintō]] *[[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] *[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]
{{translated page|ja|山王信仰|version=101692845|comment=Imported full ja history}}

This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[:en:993|993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[:en:1571|1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
== Hiyoshi/Hie Shrines Nationwide == {{Main|Sannō Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine|Hie Shrine}} === Head Shrine === * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "Shakyamuni" to "Shakyamuni Tathāgata"
revision.text:
{{One source|date=June 2023}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha nishihongu-honden.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine]] '''Sannō Faith''' (山王信仰) is a [[Shinto]] belief originating from the [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] located at the foot of [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] ([[:en:Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu City]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]]).
== Terminology == Sannō refers to another name for the deity worshipped at Hiyoshi Taisha in [[Sakamoto, Shiga|Sakamoto]], Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture, specifically indicating the deity enshrined on Mount Hiei.
Shrines named '''[[:en:Hiyoshi Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine]]''', '''[[Hie Shrine]]''', or '''Sannō Shrine''' across Japan are derived from Sannō Faith, and they enshrine the divided spirits ([[:en:Bunrei|Bunrei]]) of Hiyoshi Taisha. These shrines primarily worship [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]] and [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] (or [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]), totaling about 3,800 nationwide.
During the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] period, it was also known as '{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}' or 'Hiyoshi Sannō,' and is affectionately called '''Sannō-san''' even today. Although Hiyoshi Taisha uses [[:en:monkeys|monkeys]] as divine messengers, the exact relation with monkeys is not well understood and may reflect remnants of primitive worship.
== History == === Establishment of Hiyoshi Taisha and Enryaku-ji === Hiyoshi Taisha originally enshrined [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]], deity of Mount Hie (later named [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]). After the relocation of the capital to {{ill|Ōmi-kyō|ja|近江京|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-kyō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近江大津宮}}, in the seventh year of Emperor Tenji ([[:en:668|668]]), [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmiwa no Kami]] (Ōmononushi no Kami) was enshrined from Mount Miwa (Yamato Province) to protect the new capital.
When the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monastery of [[Enryaku-ji]] was founded on Mount Hiei, these deities became the local guardian deities. Saichō, founder of Enryaku-ji, set these local deities as the 'Various Sannō' of Mount Hiei.<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō of Hiyoshi Taisha] (Tsutomu Egashira, Iwakura Society Bulletin No. 28, July 12, 2013)</ref> The term "Sannō" was adopted following the deity "Sannō Hitsu Shinkun" enshrined at the [[:en:Tiantai Mountain|Tiantai Mountain]] Guoqing Temple in Tang China.<ref name="iwakura" />
Saichō referred to "Sannō" as a Buddhist expression of local mountain deities, avoiding explicitly calling them "kami".<ref name="iwakura" /> This approach laid the foundation for the Shinbutsu-shūgō (syncretic) "Sannō Shintō".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Ryōsho Sansei === In [[:en:825|825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchō|qq=}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch [[:en:Enchin|Enchin]] introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch [[:en:Ryōgen|Ryōgen]] elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[:en:972|972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[:en:993|993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[:en:1571|1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
== Hiyoshi/Hie Shrines Nationwide == {{Main|Sannō Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine|Hie Shrine}} === Head Shrine === * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]]
=== Notable Shrines === * [[Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|Hie Shrine]] (Tokyo) * [[Hie Shrine (Toyama)|Hie Shrine]] (Toyama)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == *{{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html |title=Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō (Tsutomu Egashira)|date=20140714221329}} == See Also == *[[:en:Sannō Shintō|Sannō Shintō]] *[[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] *[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]
{{translated page|ja|山王信仰|version=101692845|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.comment: Bot: add wikidata link templates
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11470037]] Wikidata name: Sannō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{One source|date=June 2023}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha nishihongu-honden.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine]] '''Sannō Faith''' (山王信仰) is a [[Shinto]] belief originating from the [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] located at the foot of [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] ([[:en:Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu City]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]]).
== Terminology == Sannō refers to another name for the deity worshipped at Hiyoshi Taisha in [[Sakamoto, Shiga|Sakamoto]], Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture, specifically indicating the deity enshrined on Mount Hiei.
Shrines named '''[[:en:Hiyoshi Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine]]''', '''[[Hie Shrine]]''', or '''Sannō Shrine''' across Japan are derived from Sannō Faith, and they enshrine the divided spirits ([[:en:Bunrei|Bunrei]]) of Hiyoshi Taisha. These shrines primarily worship [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]] and [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] (or [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]), totaling about 3,800 nationwide.
During the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] period, it was also known as '{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}' or 'Hiyoshi Sannō,' and is affectionately called '''Sannō-san''' even today. Although Hiyoshi Taisha uses [[:en:monkeys|monkeys]] as divine messengers, the exact relation with monkeys is not well understood and may reflect remnants of primitive worship.
== History == === Establishment of Hiyoshi Taisha and Enryaku-ji === Hiyoshi Taisha originally enshrined [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]], deity of Mount Hie (later named [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]). After the relocation of the capital to {{ill|Ōmi-kyō|ja|近江京|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-kyō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近江大津宮|1=Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638487}}, in the seventh year of Emperor Tenji ([[:en:668|668]]), [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmiwa no Kami]] (Ōmononushi no Kami) was enshrined from Mount Miwa (Yamato Province) to protect the new capital.
When the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monastery of [[Enryaku-ji]] was founded on Mount Hiei, these deities became the local guardian deities. Saichō, founder of Enryaku-ji, set these local deities as the 'Various Sannō' of Mount Hiei.<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō of Hiyoshi Taisha] (Tsutomu Egashira, Iwakura Society Bulletin No. 28, July 12, 2013)</ref> The term "Sannō" was adopted following the deity "Sannō Hitsu Shinkun" enshrined at the [[:en:Tiantai Mountain|Tiantai Mountain]] Guoqing Temple in Tang China.<ref name="iwakura" />
Saichō referred to "Sannō" as a Buddhist expression of local mountain deities, avoiding explicitly calling them "kami".<ref name="iwakura" /> This approach laid the foundation for the Shinbutsu-shūgō (syncretic) "Sannō Shintō".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Ryōsho Sansei === In [[:en:825|825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchō|qq=|1=Enchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394818}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch [[:en:Enchin|Enchin]] introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch [[:en:Ryōgen|Ryōgen]] elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[:en:972|972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[:en:993|993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[:en:1571|1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
== Hiyoshi/Hie Shrines Nationwide == {{Main|Sannō Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine|Hie Shrine}} === Head Shrine === * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]]
=== Notable Shrines === * [[Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|Hie Shrine]] (Tokyo) * [[Hie Shrine (Toyama)|Hie Shrine]] (Toyama)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == *{{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html |title=Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō (Tsutomu Egashira)|date=20140714221329}} == See Also == *[[:en:Sannō Shintō|Sannō Shintō]] *[[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] *[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]
{{translated page|ja|山王信仰|version=101692845|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Although Hiyoshi Taisha uses [[:en:monkeys|monkeys]] as divine messengers, the exact relation with monkeys is not well understood and may reflect remnants of primitive worship.

After the relocation of the capital to {{ill|Ōmi-kyō|ja|近江京|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-kyō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近江大津宮|1=Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638487}}, in the seventh year of Emperor Tenji ([[:en:668|668]]), [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmiwa no Kami]] (Ōmononushi no Kami) was enshrined from Mount Miwa (Yamato Province) to protect the new capital.
When the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monastery of [[Enryaku-ji]] was founded on Mount Hiei, these deities became the local guardian deities. Saichō, founder of Enryaku-ji, set these local deities as the 'Various Sannō' of Mount Hiei.<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō of Hiyoshi Taisha] (Tsutomu Egashira, Iwakura Society Bulletin No. 28, July 12, 2013)</ref> The term "Sannō" was adopted following the deity "Sannō Hitsu Shinkun" enshrined at the [[:en:Tiantai Mountain|Tiantai Mountain]] Guoqing Temple in Tang China.<ref name="iwakura" />
Saichō referred to "Sannō" as a Buddhist expression of local mountain deities, avoiding explicitly calling them "kami".<ref name="iwakura" /> This approach laid the foundation for the Shinbutsu-shūgō (syncretic) "Sannō Shintō".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Ryōsho Sansei === In [[:en:825|825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchō|qq=|1=Enchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394818}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch [[:en:Enchin|Enchin]] introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch [[:en:Ryōgen|Ryōgen]] elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[:en:972|972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[:en:993|993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[:en:1571|1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
== Hiyoshi/Hie Shrines Nationwide == {{Main|Sannō Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine|Hie Shrine}} === Head Shrine === * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]]
=== Notable Shrines === * [[Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|Hie Shrine]] (Tokyo) * [[Hie Shrine (Toyama)|Hie Shrine]] (Toyama)

== Bibliography == *{{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html |title=Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō (Tsutomu Egashira)|date=20140714221329}} == See Also == *[[:en:Sannō Shintō|Sannō Shintō]] *[[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] *[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:山王信仰]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11470037]] Wikidata name: Sannō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{One source|date=June 2023}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha nishihongu-honden.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine]] '''Sannō Faith''' (山王信仰) is a [[Shinto]] belief originating from the [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] located at the foot of [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] ([[:en:Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu City]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]]).
== Terminology == Sannō refers to another name for the deity worshipped at Hiyoshi Taisha in [[Sakamoto, Shiga|Sakamoto]], Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture, specifically indicating the deity enshrined on Mount Hiei.
Shrines named '''[[:en:Hiyoshi Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine]]''', '''[[Hie Shrine]]''', or '''Sannō Shrine''' across Japan are derived from Sannō Faith, and they enshrine the divided spirits ([[:en:Bunrei|Bunrei]]) of Hiyoshi Taisha. These shrines primarily worship [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]] and [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] (or [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]), totaling about 3,800 nationwide.
During the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] period, it was also known as '{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}' or 'Hiyoshi Sannō,' and is affectionately called '''Sannō-san''' even today. Although Hiyoshi Taisha uses [[:en:monkeys|monkeys]] as divine messengers, the exact relation with monkeys is not well understood and may reflect remnants of primitive worship.
== History == === Establishment of Hiyoshi Taisha and Enryaku-ji === Hiyoshi Taisha originally enshrined [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]], deity of Mount Hie (later named [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]). After the relocation of the capital to {{ill|Ōmi-kyō|ja|近江京|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-kyō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近江大津宮|1=Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638487}}, in the seventh year of Emperor Tenji ([[:en:668|668]]), [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmiwa no Kami]] (Ōmononushi no Kami) was enshrined from Mount Miwa (Yamato Province) to protect the new capital.
When the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monastery of [[Enryaku-ji]] was founded on Mount Hiei, these deities became the local guardian deities. Saichō, founder of Enryaku-ji, set these local deities as the 'Various Sannō' of Mount Hiei.<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō of Hiyoshi Taisha] (Tsutomu Egashira, Iwakura Society Bulletin No. 28, July 12, 2013)</ref> The term "Sannō" was adopted following the deity "Sannō Hitsu Shinkun" enshrined at the [[:en:Tiantai Mountain|Tiantai Mountain]] Guoqing Temple in Tang China.<ref name="iwakura" />
Saichō referred to "Sannō" as a Buddhist expression of local mountain deities, avoiding explicitly calling them "kami".<ref name="iwakura" /> This approach laid the foundation for the Shinbutsu-shūgō (syncretic) "Sannō Shintō".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Ryōsho Sansei === In [[:en:825|825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchō|1=Enchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394818}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch [[:en:Enchin|Enchin]] introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch [[:en:Ryōgen|Ryōgen]] elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[:en:972|972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[:en:993|993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[:en:1571|1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.

This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[:en:993|993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[:en:1571|1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
== Hiyoshi/Hie Shrines Nationwide == {{Main|Sannō Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine|Hie Shrine}} === Head Shrine === * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]]
=== Notable Shrines === * [[Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|Hie Shrine]] (Tokyo) * [[Hie Shrine (Toyama)|Hie Shrine]] (Toyama)

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:山王信仰]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11470037]] Wikidata name: Sannō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{One source|date=June 2023}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha nishihongu-honden.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine]] '''Sannō Faith''' (山王信仰) is a [[Shinto]] belief originating from the [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] located at the foot of [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] ([[:en:Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu City]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]]).
== Terminology == Sannō refers to another name for the deity worshipped at Hiyoshi Taisha in [[Sakamoto, Shiga|Sakamoto]], Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture, specifically indicating the deity enshrined on Mount Hiei.
Shrines named '''[[:en:Hiyoshi Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine]]''', '''[[Hie Shrine]]''', or '''Sannō Shrine''' across Japan are derived from Sannō Faith, and they enshrine the divided spirits ([[:en:Bunrei|Bunrei]]) of Hiyoshi Taisha. These shrines primarily worship [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]] and [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] (or [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]), totaling about 3,800 nationwide.
During the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] period, it was also known as '{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}' or 'Hiyoshi Sannō,' and is affectionately called '''Sannō-san''' even today. Although Hiyoshi Taisha uses [[:en:monkeys|monkeys]] as divine messengers, the exact relation with monkeys is not well understood and may reflect remnants of primitive worship.
== History == === Establishment of Hiyoshi Taisha and Enryaku-ji === Hiyoshi Taisha originally enshrined [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]], deity of Mount Hie (later named [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]). After the relocation of the capital to {{ill|Ōmi-kyō|ja|近江京|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-kyō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近江大津宮|1=Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638487}}, in the seventh year of Emperor Tenji ([[:en:668|668]]), [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmiwa no Kami]] (Ōmononushi no Kami) was enshrined from Mount Miwa (Yamato Province) to protect the new capital.
When the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monastery of [[Enryaku-ji]] was founded on Mount Hiei, these deities became the local guardian deities. Saichō, founder of Enryaku-ji, set these local deities as the 'Various Sannō' of Mount Hiei.<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō of Hiyoshi Taisha] (Tsutomu Egashira, Iwakura Society Bulletin No. 28, July 12, 2013)</ref> The term "Sannō" was adopted following the deity "Sannō Hitsu Shinkun" enshrined at the [[:en:Tiantai Mountain|Tiantai Mountain]] Guoqing Temple in Tang China.<ref name="iwakura" />
Saichō referred to "Sannō" as a Buddhist expression of local mountain deities, avoiding explicitly calling them "kami".<ref name="iwakura" /> This approach laid the foundation for the Shinbutsu-shūgō (syncretic) "Sannō Shintō".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Ryōsho Sansei === In [[:en:825|825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchō|1=Enchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394818}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch [[:en:Enchin|Enchin]] introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch [[:en:Ryōgen|Ryōgen]] elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[:en:972|972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[:en:993|993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[:en:1571|1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
== Hiyoshi/Hie Shrines Nationwide == {{Main|Sannō Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine|Hie Shrine}} === Head Shrine === * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]]
=== Notable Shrines === * [[Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|Hie Shrine]] (Tokyo) * [[Hie Shrine (Toyama)|Hie Shrine]] (Toyama)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == *{{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html |title=Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō (Tsutomu Egashira)|date=20140714221329}} == See Also == *[[:en:Sannō Shintō|Sannō Shintō]] *[[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] *[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]

Saichō, founder of Enryaku-ji, set these local deities as the 'Various Sannō' of Mount Hiei.<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō of Hiyoshi Taisha] (Tsutomu Egashira, Iwakura Society Bulletin No. 28, July 12, 2013)</ref> The term "Sannō" was adopted following the deity "Sannō Hitsu Shinkun" enshrined at the [[:en:Tiantai Mountain|Tiantai Mountain]] Guoqing Temple in Tang China.<ref name="iwakura" />

[[ja:山王信仰]] [[Category:Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11470037}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:山王信仰]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11470037]] Wikidata name: Sannō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{One source|date=June 2023}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha nishihongu-honden.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine]] '''Sannō Faith''' (山王信仰) is a [[Shinto]] belief originating from the [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] located at the foot of [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] ([[:en:Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu City]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]]).
== Terminology == Sannō refers to another name for the deity worshipped at Hiyoshi Taisha in [[Sakamoto, Shiga|Sakamoto]], Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture, specifically indicating the deity enshrined on Mount Hiei.
Shrines named '''[[:en:Hiyoshi Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine]]''', '''[[Hie Shrine]]''', or '''Sannō Shrine''' across Japan are derived from Sannō Faith, and they enshrine the divided spirits ([[:en:Bunrei|Bunrei]]) of Hiyoshi Taisha. These shrines primarily worship [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]] and [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] (or [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]), totaling about 3,800 nationwide.
During the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] period, it was also known as '{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}' or 'Hiyoshi Sannō,' and is affectionately called '''Sannō-san''' even today. Although Hiyoshi Taisha uses [[:en:monkeys|monkeys]] as divine messengers, the exact relation with monkeys is not well understood and may reflect remnants of primitive worship.
== History == === Establishment of Hiyoshi Taisha and Enryaku-ji === Hiyoshi Taisha originally enshrined [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]], deity of Mount Hie (later named [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]). After the relocation of the capital to {{ill|Ōmi-kyō|ja|近江京|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-kyō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近江大津宮|1=Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638487}}, in the seventh year of Emperor Tenji ([[:en:668|668]]), [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmiwa no Kami]] (Ōmononushi no Kami) was enshrined from Mount Miwa (Yamato Province) to protect the new capital.
When the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monastery of [[Enryaku-ji]] was founded on Mount Hiei, these deities became the local guardian deities. Saichō, founder of Enryaku-ji, set these local deities as the 'Various Sannō' of Mount Hiei.<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō of Hiyoshi Taisha] (Tsutomu Egashira, Iwakura Society Bulletin No. 28, July 12, 2013)</ref> The term "Sannō" was adopted following the deity "Sannō Hitsu Shinkun" enshrined at the [[:en:Tiantai Mountain|Tiantai Mountain]] Guoqing Temple in Tang China.<ref name="iwakura" />
Saichō referred to "Sannō" as a Buddhist expression of local mountain deities, avoiding explicitly calling them "kami".<ref name="iwakura" /> This approach laid the foundation for the Shinbutsu-shūgō (syncretic) "Sannō Shintō".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Ryōsho Sansei === In [[:en:825|825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchō|1=Enchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394818}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch [[:en:Enchin|Enchin]] introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch [[:en:Ryōgen|Ryōgen]] elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[:en:972|972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[:en:993|993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[:en:1571|1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
== Hiyoshi/Hie Shrines Nationwide == {{Main|Sannō Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine|Hie Shrine}} === Head Shrine === * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]]
=== Notable Shrines === * [[Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|Hie Shrine]] (Tokyo) * [[Hie Shrine (Toyama)|Hie Shrine]] (Toyama)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == *{{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html |title=Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō (Tsutomu Egashira)|date=20140714221329}} == See Also == *[[:en:Sannō Shintō|Sannō Shintō]] *[[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] *[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]
{{translated page|ja|山王信仰|version=101692845|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Although Hiyoshi Taisha uses [[:en:monkeys|monkeys]] as divine messengers, the exact relation with monkeys is not well understood and may reflect remnants of primitive worship.
== History == === Establishment of Hiyoshi Taisha and Enryaku-ji === Hiyoshi Taisha originally enshrined [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]], deity of Mount Hie (later named [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]). After the relocation of the capital to {{ill|Ōmi-kyō|ja|近江京|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi-kyō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近江大津宮|1=Ōmi Ōtsu Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638487}}, in the seventh year of Emperor Tenji ([[:en:668|668]]), [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmiwa no Kami]] (Ōmononushi no Kami) was enshrined from Mount Miwa (Yamato Province) to protect the new capital.
When the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monastery of [[Enryaku-ji]] was founded on Mount Hiei, these deities became the local guardian deities. Saichō, founder of Enryaku-ji, set these local deities as the 'Various Sannō' of Mount Hiei.<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō of Hiyoshi Taisha] (Tsutomu Egashira, Iwakura Society Bulletin No. 28, July 12, 2013)</ref> The term "Sannō" was adopted following the deity "Sannō Hitsu Shinkun" enshrined at the [[:en:Tiantai Mountain|Tiantai Mountain]] Guoqing Temple in Tang China.<ref name="iwakura" />
Saichō referred to "Sannō" as a Buddhist expression of local mountain deities, avoiding explicitly calling them "kami".<ref name="iwakura" /> This approach laid the foundation for the Shinbutsu-shūgō (syncretic) "Sannō Shintō".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Ryōsho Sansei === In [[:en:825|825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchō|1=Enchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394818}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch [[:en:Enchin|Enchin]] introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch [[:en:Ryōgen|Ryōgen]] elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[:en:972|972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[:en:993|993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[:en:1571|1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
== Hiyoshi/Hie Shrines Nationwide == {{Main|Sannō Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine|Hie Shrine}} === Head Shrine === * [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]]
=== Notable Shrines === * [[Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|Hie Shrine]] (Tokyo) * [[Hie Shrine (Toyama)|Hie Shrine]] (Toyama)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == *{{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html |title=Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō (Tsutomu Egashira)|date=20140714221329}} == See Also == *[[:en:Sannō Shintō|Sannō Shintō]] *[[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] *[[Shinbutsu-shūgō]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:山王信仰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:山王信仰]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11470037]] Wikidata name: Sannō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:06, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{One source|date=June 2023}} [[File:Hiyoshi-taisha nishihongu-honden.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Hiyoshi Taisha Shrine]] '''Sannō Faith''' (山王信仰) is a [[Shinto]] belief originating from the [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]] located at the foot of [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] ([[:en:Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu City]], [[:en:Shiga Prefecture|Shiga Prefecture]]).
== Terminology == Sannō refers to another name for the deity worshipped at Hiyoshi Taisha in [[Sakamoto, Shiga|Sakamoto]], Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture, specifically indicating the deity enshrined on Mount Hiei.
Shrines named '''[[:en:Hiyoshi Shrine|Hiyoshi Shrine]]''', '''[[Hie Shrine]]''', or '''Sannō Shrine''' across Japan are derived from Sannō Faith, and they enshrine the divided spirits ([[:en:Bunrei|Bunrei]]) of Hiyoshi Taisha. These shrines primarily worship [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]] and [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] (or [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]]), totaling about 3,800 nationwide.
During the [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] period, it was also known as '{{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}' or 'Hiyoshi Sannō,' and is affectionately called '''Sannō-san''' even today. Although Hiyoshi Taisha uses [[:en:monkeys|monkeys]] as divine messengers, the exact relation with monkeys is not well understood and may reflect remnants of primitive worship.
== History == === Establishment of Hiyoshi Taisha and Enryaku-ji === Hiyoshi Taisha originally enshrined [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ōyamakui no Kami]], deity of Mount Hie (later named [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]]). When the [[:en:Tiantai|Tendai]] monastery of [[Enryaku-ji]] was founded on Mount Hiei, these deities became the local guardian deities. Saichō, founder of Enryaku-ji, set these local deities as the 'Various Sannō' of Mount Hiei.<ref name="iwakura">[https://web.archive.org/web/20140714221329/http://www.geocities.jp/yamauo1945/sannou3sei.html Formation of the Three Sacred Sannō of Hiyoshi Taisha] (Tsutomu Egashira, Iwakura Society Bulletin No. 28, July 12, 2013)</ref> The term "Sannō" was adopted following the deity "Sannō Hitsu Shinkun" enshrined at the [[:en:Tiantai Mountain|Tiantai Mountain]] Guoqing Temple in Tang China.<ref name="iwakura" />
Saichō referred to "Sannō" as a Buddhist expression of local mountain deities, avoiding explicitly calling them "kami".<ref name="iwakura" /> This approach laid the foundation for the Shinbutsu-shūgō (syncretic) "Sannō Shintō".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Ryōsho Sansei === In [[:en:825|825]], second Tendai patriarch {{ill|Enchō|ja|円澄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchō|1=Enchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394818}} established the West Pagoda at Enryaku-ji, venerating a distinct deity called Obie Deity (小比叡神). Thus, the 'Various Sannō' split into East Pagoda (Daibie Deity 大比叡神) and West Pagoda deities.<ref name="iwakura" />
Later, fifth patriarch [[:en:Enchin|Enchin]] introduced a personal deity, "Sannō Myōjin," worshipped after receiving revelations in dreams, creating a triad called "Ryōsho Sansei".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch [[:en:Ryōgen|Ryōgen]] elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[:en:972|972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[:en:993|993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[:en:1571|1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine

=== Jinushi Sansei === Eighteenth patriarch [[:en:Ryōgen|Ryōgen]] elevated the Yokawa area to parity with East and West Pagodas in [[:en:972|972]], introducing a distinct deity, Shōshinshi (聖真子). This formed another triad: Daibie (East), Obie (West), and Shōshinshi (Yokawa), termed "Jinushi Sansei."<ref name="iwakura" />
=== From Jinushi Sansei to Sannō Sansei === "Jinushi Sansei" gradually evolved into "Sannō Sansei," becoming prominent after the Enryaku-ji split in [[:en:993|993]].<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Expansion of Sannō Faith === With Tendai Buddhism spreading nationwide, Hiyoshi Shrines multiplied, influencing regional place names such as Hiyoshi, Hie, Hie, Sannō, and Sakamoto.
=== Honji-butsu Establishment === Each deity received a honji-butsu (original Buddha form): Daibie as Shaka Nyorai, Obie as Yakushi Nyorai, and Shōshinshi as Amida Nyorai. Additional shrines eventually formed the "Sannō Twenty-One Shrines".<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Sannō Shintō === Sannō Shintō considers Sannō as a manifestation of Shakyamuni Tathāgata, symbolically linking the characters for "mountain" (山) and "king" (王) to Tendai teachings.<ref name="iwakura" />
=== Medieval Period and Later === Sannō shrines became involved in warrior monks' protests. Destroyed in [[:en:1571|1571]] by Oda Nobunaga, Hiyoshi Taisha was later rebuilt.
;Upper Seven Shrines * Nishihongū (Daibie) * Higashihongū (Obie) * Usa no Miya (Shōshinshi) * Ushio Shrine (Hachiōji) * Hakusan Shrine (Marōdo) * Juge Shrine (Jūzenji) * Sannomiya Shrine
;Middle Seven Shrines * Ōmonoimi Shrine * Ushi no Miko Shrine * Shinmonoimi Shrine * Yahashira Shrine * Hayao Shrine * Ubuya Shrine * Usa Wakamiya Shrine
;Lower Seven Shrines * Juge Wakamiya * Kamadodono Shrine (Ōmiya) * Kamadodono Shrine (Ninomiya) * Ujigami Shrine * Iwataki Shrine * Tsurugi no Miya Shrine * Kehi Shrine
In Edo, the Sannō Festival became a major celebration following the relocation of a local Hiyoshi shrine by Tokugawa Ieyasu.
